title: Giant structures — diamond, graphite and why they're so different
examTip: Diamond and graphite are both pure carbon — same element, completely different structures, completely different properties. The exam loves asking you to explain the difference. Always link each property back to the bonding and structure.
introTips: Diamond is the hardest natural material on Earth. Graphite is so soft it rubs off onto paper — that's what pencil 'lead' actually is.
Both are made of nothing but carbon atoms. The only difference is HOW those atoms are bonded together.
This topic shows you that structure determines properties — and that's one of the most powerful ideas in all of chemistry.
intro: Imagine two materials: one is the hardest substance found in nature, doesn't conduct electricity, and melts above 3500°C. The other is so slippery it's used as a lubricant, conducts electricity, and rubs off onto paper when you write. Now imagine they're both made of exactly the same thing: carbon atoms. That seems impossible — but it's true. Diamond and graphite are both pure carbon, and the extraordinary difference in their properties comes down entirely to how the carbon atoms are arranged and bonded. This is what giant covalent structures are all about — and once you understand it, you'll also understand ionic lattices, metallic bonding and why silicon dioxide is so hard, all from the same core idea.

keyPoints.heading: Giant ionic structures
keyPoints.content: Ionic compounds form giant ionic lattices — a regular, repeating three-dimensional arrangement of millions of oppositely charged ions. The lattice is held together by strong electrostatic forces between the positive and negative ions acting in all directions. This structure gives ionic compounds several characteristic properties: High melting and boiling points — a huge number of strong electrostatic forces must be overcome simultaneously; requires a lot of energy. Hard but brittle — the lattice resists deformation (hard), but when struck, layers shift so that ions of the same charge come next to each other; like charges repel, and the lattice shatters. Conduct electricity only when molten or dissolved in water — in the solid state, ions are fixed in position and cannot carry charge. When melted or dissolved, ions become free to move and carry charge. Dissolve in water — water molecules attract the ions and pull them away from the lattice. Examples: NaCl (sodium chloride), MgO (magnesium oxide — higher melting point than NaCl because Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges than Na⁺ and Cl⁻, so stronger electrostatic forces).
writeBullets: Giant ionic lattice: regular 3D arrangement of millions of oppositely charged ions
Held by strong electrostatic forces in all directions
High melting points: many strong forces must be overcome simultaneously
Hard but brittle: lattice resists force, but shatters when like charges align
Conduct electricity only when molten or dissolved (ions must be free to move)
Dissolve in water; MgO has higher mp than NaCl (higher charges = stronger forces)

keyTerms.def: A regular 3D arrangement of millions of oppositely charged ions held together by electrostatic forces
The attractive force between oppositely charged particles (positive and negative ions in an ionic lattice)
Shatters when struck, because shifting layers bring like charges together, which repel and cause fracture

keyPoints.memTip: Ionic compounds: SOLID = fixed ions = can't conduct. LIQUID/DISSOLVED = free ions = can conduct. Always link conductivity to whether the ions can MOVE.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The brittleness explanation (like charges align → repel → shatter) is a high-value answer that distinguishes between ionic and metallic structures. Contrast with metallic bonding where layers slide without the bonding breaking.

keyPoints.heading: Diamond — the hardest giant covalent structure
keyPoints.content: In diamond, each carbon atom forms 4 covalent bonds to 4 other carbon atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement. This creates a rigid, 3D lattice extending throughout the entire structure — every bond you could possibly break is itself supported by more bonds in every direction. Properties explained by this structure: Extremely hard (the hardest natural substance) — to deform diamond you would have to break strong covalent bonds; the structure resists in all directions simultaneously. Very high melting point (above 3500°C) — many thousands of strong covalent bonds must be broken to melt it; requires enormous energy. Does not conduct electricity — all four outer electrons of each carbon atom are used in covalent bonds; there are no free electrons or ions to carry charge. Insoluble in water — the strong covalent bonds are not broken by water molecules. Uses: cutting tools (diamond-tipped drills and saws exploit its hardness), gemstones (exploits optical properties and hardness), abrasives (grinding and polishing hard materials).
writeBullets: Each C atom bonded to 4 others in a tetrahedral arrangement
Rigid 3D lattice — covalent bonds extend throughout the whole structure
Hardest natural substance: every bond must break to deform it
Very high melting point (>3500°C): thousands of strong covalent bonds to break
Does not conduct electricity: all electrons are in bonds (none delocalised)
Uses: cutting tools, gemstones, abrasives

keyTerms.def: A structure where atoms are held together by covalent bonds that extend throughout the whole material, with no separate molecules
A 3D arrangement where each atom is bonded to 4 others at 109.5° — the geometry of diamond's carbon atoms

keyPoints.memTip: Diamond: FOUR bonds, RIGID structure, NO conduction. Graphite: THREE bonds, LAYERS, CAN conduct. The bond count (3 vs 4) is the key to remembering the difference.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say 'diamond doesn't conduct because it has no ions' — technically true but incomplete. The AQA mark scheme wants: 'no free electrons' (all electrons are in covalent bonds). Make this distinction.

keyPoints.heading: Graphite — the conducting non-metal
keyPoints.content: In graphite, each carbon atom forms only 3 covalent bonds, arranged in flat hexagonal rings. These hexagons form flat layers (like a honeycomb). Each carbon has one electron left over that is not used in bonding — this electron becomes delocalised and can move freely between the layers. The layers themselves are held together by weak intermolecular forces (van der Waals forces). Properties: Conducts electricity — delocalised electrons move freely parallel to the layers; graphite is the only non-metal that conducts electricity. Soft and slippery — the layers slide over each other easily because the forces between layers are weak; this makes graphite useful as a lubricant and as the 'lead' in pencils (layers rub off onto paper). High melting point — the covalent bonds within each layer are very strong; huge energy needed to break them. Insoluble in water. Graphene: a single layer of graphite. Extremely strong (stronger than steel per unit weight), excellent electrical conductor, transparent, only one atom thick — potential uses in electronics, flexible screens, composites. Fullerenes: carbon molecules shaped as hollow spheres or tubes. C₆₀ (buckminsterfullerene) is a sphere of 60 carbon atoms; carbon nanotubes are cylindrical. Uses: drug delivery, nanotechnology, catalysts, composite materials.
writeBullets: Each C atom bonded to 3 others in flat hexagonal layers
One electron per carbon is delocalised → conducts electricity (unique non-metal conductor)
Layers held by weak intermolecular forces → slide easily → soft, slippery
High melting point: strong covalent bonds WITHIN layers (not between layers)
Graphene = single graphite layer; extremely strong, excellent conductor
Fullerenes (C₆₀): hollow spheres; used in drug delivery and nanotechnology

keyTerms.def: A giant covalent structure of carbon with flat hexagonal layers; conducts electricity via delocalised electrons
A single layer of graphite; extremely strong and an excellent electrical conductor
A carbon molecule in the form of a hollow sphere or tube (e.g. C₆₀ buckminsterfullerene)
Weak intermolecular forces that hold the layers of graphite together

keyPoints.memTip: Graphite has LAYERS that SLIDE. Diamond has RIGID bonds in ALL directions. Both have high melting points (strong covalent bonds within the structure), but only graphite conducts (delocalised electrons).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Graphite conducting electricity is a classic AQA question. The answer must mention: 'each carbon atom has one delocalised electron that is free to move between the layers'. Just saying 'free electrons' won't score the mark without explaining where they come from.

keyPoints.heading: Comparing giant structures — ionic, covalent and metallic
keyPoints.content: All three giant structures — ionic (NaCl, MgO), giant covalent (diamond, graphite, SiO₂), and metallic (iron, copper, aluminium) — share some features: all have strong forces throughout the structure, all have high melting and boiling points, all are solids at room temperature (with exceptions like mercury). The key differences are in electrical conductivity and physical properties: Metallic: conducts electricity always (delocalised electrons). Ionic: conducts only when molten or dissolved (ions must be free). Graphite: conducts (delocalised electrons). Diamond, SiO₂: never conducts (no free electrons or ions). Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) — also called silica or quartz — is a giant covalent structure like diamond. Each silicon atom is bonded to 4 oxygen atoms, and each oxygen is bonded to 2 silicon atoms. Very hard, very high melting point (1710°C), doesn't conduct electricity. Used in glass-making and as quartz in electronics. Contrast with simple molecular compounds (like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄) — these have weak intermolecular forces between molecules → low melting points, don't conduct electricity.
writeBullets: All giant structures: high melting/boiling points; strong forces throughout
Metallic: conducts always (delocalised electrons)
Ionic: conducts only when molten or dissolved (ions free to move)
Graphite: conducts (delocalised electrons); diamond and SiO₂: never conduct
SiO₂ (quartz): each Si bonded to 4 O; very hard, high mp, no conductivity
Simple molecular compounds: weak intermolecular forces → low mp; no conductivity

keyTerms.def: A giant covalent structure; very hard, high melting point, doesn't conduct electricity; found as quartz
A compound made of individual molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces; low melting point

keyPoints.memTip: Conductivity quick guide: METAL always conducts. IONIC conducts when LIQUID or dissolved. DIAMOND/SiO₂ never conducts. GRAPHITE always conducts (exception to the 'covalent = no conduction' rule).
keyPoints.tutor_note: The comparison table is worth spending time on. In higher-tier papers, students are often asked to explain anomalies — graphite conducting, or why MgO has a higher melting point than NaCl. Both need structural explanations.

tables.title: Comparing giant structures — ionic, covalent and metallic
rows: Covalent within layers (3 bonds); weak forces between layers

0.question: Sodium chloride (NaCl) conducts electricity when dissolved in water but not when solid. Why?
options: When dissolved, the ions are free to move and carry charge; in the solid, ions are fixed in the lattice
When dissolved, electrons are released and become free to move
Water breaks the covalent bonds in NaCl, releasing electrons
The lattice structure collapses when heated, allowing electrons to flow
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right! In solid NaCl, the ions are locked in the lattice and cannot move — so no charge can flow. When dissolved in water, the water molecules pull the ions apart, so Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions are free to move through the solution and carry electrical charge.
0.wrongFeedback: Remember: ionic compounds conduct through ION movement, not electron movement (that's metals). In solid NaCl, the ions are fixed and can't move. When dissolved or molten, the ions become free to move through the liquid and carry charge.

1.question: Diamond and graphite are both made only of carbon atoms. Which statement correctly explains why graphite conducts electricity but diamond does not?
options: In graphite, each carbon uses only 3 of its 4 outer electrons in bonding, leaving 1 delocalised electron per atom that can move freely
Graphite has ionic bonds between its layers that allow charge to flow
Diamond has more carbon atoms per unit volume, blocking electron movement
Graphite contains impurities that make it conductive
1.correctFeedback: Spot on! In graphite, each carbon atom forms 3 bonds — leaving 1 electron per atom delocalised and free to move between the layers. In diamond, all 4 outer electrons are used in bonds — there are no free electrons to carry charge. Same element, completely different electron arrangement.
1.wrongFeedback: Both diamond and graphite are pure carbon with covalent bonds. The difference is in how many electrons are used for bonding. Diamond: all 4 outer electrons bonded (none free). Graphite: only 3 outer electrons bonded, so 1 per atom is delocalised and can carry charge.

3.question: Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) has a very high melting point and does not conduct electricity. What type of structure is this?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! SiO₂ is a giant covalent structure — atoms bonded covalently throughout the whole structure. This gives it a very high melting point (lots of strong covalent bonds to break) and no electrical conductivity (no free electrons or ions).
3.wrongFeedback: Think about the evidence: very high melting point (many strong bonds throughout the structure) + no conductivity (no free electrons or ions). This combination is characteristic of a giant covalent structure — like diamond. Simple molecular structures have LOW melting points; ionic structures conduct when molten.

commonMistakes: Saying graphite conducts because it has 'free electrons' without explaining WHY — you must say that each carbon forms 3 bonds, leaving 1 electron per atom delocalised. The source of the electrons matters to AQA.
Saying ionic compounds conduct in the solid state — they cannot. The ions are fixed in the lattice. Conductivity requires free-moving charge carriers (molten or dissolved ionic compounds, or metals).
Confusing 'high melting point' as the explanation for hardness — they often go together but they're different properties. High melting point = strong forces; hardness = resistance to deformation.

Saying graphite has a low melting point because the layers slide easily — the weak forces are between layers, but the covalent bonds WITHIN layers are very strong. Graphite has a very high melting point.

Forgetting that simple molecular substances (H₂O, CO₂, sugar) have LOW melting points — don't confuse having covalent bonds (within molecules) with being a giant covalent structure (covalent bonds throughout).
examTips: The most common exam question: 'Explain why graphite is soft but diamond is hard.' Structure your answer: Diamond — 4 covalent bonds, rigid 3D lattice, must break bonds to deform → very hard. Graphite — 3 bonds, layers with weak forces between them, layers slide over each other → soft.
For MgO vs NaCl melting point comparison: MgO has higher charges (Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ vs Na⁺ and Cl⁻) → stronger electrostatic forces → higher melting point. One sentence, full marks.

When comparing giant and simple molecular structures: giant structures have many strong bonds throughout (high melting point); simple molecules have weak intermolecular forces between individual molecules (low melting point).

For graphene and fullerene questions: state the structure (single graphite layer / hollow carbon spheres or tubes), then link to the property being asked about (strength, conductivity, size for drug delivery etc.).
revisionCardBullets: Giant ionic lattice: strong electrostatic forces; high mp; hard but brittle; conducts when molten/dissolved
Brittle: striking shifts layers so like charges align → repel → shatter
Diamond: each C bonded to 4 others; tetrahedral; hardest natural substance
Diamond: very high mp; no conductivity (all electrons in bonds); insoluble
Graphite: each C bonded to 3 others; flat hexagonal layers
Graphite: 1 delocalised e per C → only non-metal that conducts electricity
Graphite: weak forces between layers → layers slide → soft and slippery
Graphite: high mp (strong covalent bonds within layers)
Graphene = single graphite layer; extremely strong; excellent conductor
Fullerenes: C₆₀ (buckminsterfullerene); hollow spheres; drug delivery, catalysts
SiO₂ (quartz): giant covalent, each Si bonded to 4 O; very hard; no conductivity
Conductivity summary: metal always; ionic when molten/dissolved; graphite yes; diamond/SiO₂ never
